acceptance: FEAT-001 has acceptance criteria covering all three loop-detection sentinels; each AC references the existing tests as verification; no code changes required
description:
FEAT-001-agent-loop.md acceptance criteria (AC-FEAT-001-01 through AC-FEAT-001-06) do not cover the three loop-detection behaviors shipped in v0.3.1:

1. ErrReasoningOverflow: stream aborted when model emits >32k chars of pure reasoning_content with no content/tool_call delta
2. ErrReasoningStall: stream aborted when only reasoning_content deltas arrive for >reasoningStallTimeout with no forward progress
3. ErrToolCallLoop: loop aborted when identical tool-call set repeats >=3 consecutive turns

Implementation: loop.go (toolCallLoopLimit, toolCallFingerprint, ErrToolCallLoop handling), stream_consume.go (reasoningByteLimit, stall timer, ErrReasoningOverflow/Stall), errors.go
Tests: TestRun_ToolCallLoopDetection, TestRun_ToolCallLoopCounterResetsOnDifferentCall (loop_test.go); TestConsumeStream_ReasoningOverflow, TestConsumeStream_ReasoningStall (stream_consume_test.go)

Work: Add AC-FEAT-001-07, AC-FEAT-001-08, AC-FEAT-001-09 (or equivalent) to FEAT-001 covering these three behaviors. Reference the existing tests as verification evidence. Discovered by alignment review agent-35bf3d08, review issue agent-0ac72188.

Resolution direction: plan-to-code (code already correct; planning must catch up)
title: docs: add loop-detection acceptance criteria to FEAT-001

acceptance: go test -race ./... passes; Tool interface or wrapper gains a Parallel bool indicator; read/glob/grep/ls are marked Parallel=true; write/edit/patch/bash/task are Parallel=false; TestRun_ParallelToolExecution exists in loop_test.go and verifies concurrent dispatch of parallel-flagged tools; Result.ToolCalls preserves original provider-returned order
description:
<context-digest>
<principles>Design for Change · Design for Simplicity · Validate Your Work · Make Intent Explicit · Prefer Reversible Decisions</principles>
<concerns>go-std | testing</concerns>
<practices>gofmt · go vet · golangci-lint · gosec · govulncheck · go test · go test -race · testify/assert for assertions</practices>
<governing>SD-001 §D4 — tools implement the Tool interface with Name(), Description(), Schema(), Execute(). SD-001 §Business Rules rule 2 — "Tool execution is sequential" (this bead changes that rule for Parallel-flagged tools). FEAT-001 AC-FEAT-001-02 — tool calls execute in provider order and record each call.</governing>
</context-digest>

Currently loop.go executes all tool calls sequentially regardless of whether they could safely run in parallel. Codex uses an explicit per-tool parallel flag to run independent tools concurrently, reducing wall time.

What to change:

1. agent.go (Tool interface or Tool struct): Add Parallel bool field to the Tool interface or to a ToolOptions/ToolMeta struct embedded in Tool. The simplest approach: add a Parallel() bool method to the Tool interface, or add a Parallel bool to a concrete ToolDef wrapper. Choose whichever fits the existing pattern best.

2. tool/ built-ins: Mark read, glob, grep, ls as Parallel: true (read-only, safe to parallelize). Mark write, edit, patch, bash, task as Parallel: false (stateful/side-effects, must serialize).

3. loop.go tool execution: When a provider response contains multiple tool calls, partition them into parallel and serial groups. Execute all consecutive parallel tools as a group using goroutines + errgroup (or sync.WaitGroup). Execute serial tools one at a time. Preserve the original order in Result.ToolCalls (sort by provider-returned order, not completion order).

4. Tests: Add TestRun_ParallelToolExecution in loop_test.go verifying parallel-flagged tools execute concurrently (use a mock provider that returns 3 parallel tool calls in one response; use sync.WaitGroup or channel timing to confirm concurrent dispatch). go test -race ./... must pass.

Governing specs: SD-001, FEAT-001, FEAT-002.
title: feat: per-tool Parallel flag with concurrent tool execution

acceptance:
go test ./tool/... passes; TestBashTool_OutputFormat (new or updated) asserts that bash Execute() returns a string matching "Exit code: N
Wall time: ...
Output:
..." for both successful and failing commands; existing bash tests updated to match new format
description:
<context-digest>
<principles>Design for Change · Design for Simplicity · Validate Your Work · Make Intent Explicit · Prefer Reversible Decisions</principles>
<concerns>go-std | testing</concerns>
<practices>gofmt · go vet · golangci-lint · gosec · govulncheck · go test · go test -race · testify/assert for assertions</practices>
<governing>SD-001 §D4 — tools implement the Tool interface; Execute() returns a string result that is inserted into the conversation as a tool result message. FEAT-002 AC-FEAT-002-01 — bash implements documented core semantics including capturing stdout, stderr, and exit code.</governing>
</context-digest>

Currently tool/bash.go returns raw combined output as the tool result string. Codex normalizes shell output into a human-readable structured format before inserting it into the prompt, improving model comprehension of tool results.

What to change in tool/bash.go:
- Format the Execute() return value as structured text instead of raw output:
  Exit code: <N>
  Wall time: <duration>s
  Output:
  <stdout/stderr>
- Use the existing wall-time measurement already tracked in bash.go for the duration field.
- Keep the existing output truncation (TruncateTail) applied to the Output section — truncation happens before formatting.
- If the command produced no output, the Output section should read "(no output)".
- Update bash_test.go to assert the new format.

Governing specs: SD-001, FEAT-002.
title: feat: structured tool output format for bash results

acceptance: go test -tags=integration ./provider/openai/... passes when vidar is reachable; tests skip cleanly when providers are unavailable
description:
The testing concern specifies provider integration tests against real LM Studio and real APIs using build tags. provider/openai/ has no integration-tagged tests today — all coverage is mock-based.

New file: provider/openai/discovery_integration_test.go
Build tag: //go:build integration

Required tests:

1. TestIntegration_LookupModelLimits_LMStudio
   - Probe vidar:1234 (or LMSTUDIO_URL) with flavor='lmstudio'
   - Assert limits.ContextLength > 0
   - Assert loaded_context_length is preferred over max_context_length when both present

2. TestIntegration_LookupModelLimits_Omlx
   - Probe vidar:1235 (or OMLX_URL) with flavor='omlx'
   - Assert limits.ContextLength > 0
   - Assert limits.MaxCompletionTokens > 0 (omlx exposes max_tokens, LM Studio does not)

3. TestIntegration_ProbeProviderFlavor_LMStudio
   - Probe vidar:1234 with NO flavor hint
   - Assert result == 'lmstudio'

4. TestIntegration_ProbeProviderFlavor_Omlx
   - Probe vidar:1235 with NO flavor hint
   - Assert result == 'omlx'

5. TestIntegration_DiscoverModels_LMStudio
   - Call DiscoverModels against vidar:1234
   - Assert len(ids) > 0

6. TestIntegration_DiscoverModels_Omlx
   - Call DiscoverModels against vidar:1235
   - Assert len(ids) > 0

7. TestIntegration_OmlxToolCalling (NEW)
   - Send a simple Chat() request with one tool definition to vidar:1235
   - Use a Qwen3.5 model (omlx translates its XML <function=...> format server-side)
   - Assert response.ToolCalls is non-empty (not leaked into response.Content)
   - Assert tool call has non-empty Name and valid JSON Arguments
   - Rationale: omlx docs say structured tool calls are emitted after parsing the completed
     turn, server-side. This test verifies the translation actually produces standard
     OpenAI tool_calls and does not leak raw XML markup into the content field.

Helper pattern (mirror lmStudioURL in integration_test.go):
- omlxURL(t) checks OMLX_URL env, then tries vidar:1235, localhost:1235; t.Skip if unreachable
- lmStudioURLForDiscovery(t) checks LMSTUDIO_URL env, then tries vidar:1234, bragi:1234; t.Skip if unreachable

OpenRouter tests (optional / separate): covered adequately by mock since the API is public and stable; skip for now unless a real OPENROUTER_API_KEY is available in test env.
title: test: integration tests for LookupModelLimits and probeProviderFlavor against live providers

Preserve existing JSONL replay behavior while adding trace correlation and root/session rollups.

acceptance: Provider tests covering AC-FEAT-003-02, AC-FEAT-003-03, and AC-FEAT-003-04 exist and prove interrupted-stream behavior, endpoint error clarity/time bounds, and call-time auth failure semantics; the provider and default Go test suites pass.
description:
<context-digest>
<principles>Provider contracts should distinguish transport failures from model behavior · Constructor-vs-call-time semantics need explicit tests because they affect config loading and speculative provider creation</principles>
<concerns>stream interruption | endpoint errors | call-time auth failures</concerns>
<practices>Use `httptest` servers and short contexts for provider failure-path tests · Assert surfaced error strings include the attempted endpoint/base URL</practices>
<adrs>ADR-001 for attempt metadata expectations</adrs>
<governing>FEAT-003 AC-FEAT-003-02, AC-FEAT-003-03, and AC-FEAT-003-04</governing>
</context-digest>

Provider tests already cover single-attempt behavior and some metadata, but they do not fully encode the remaining edge contracts. Add tests that:
- force interrupted/partial streaming responses in each provider package and prove partial response state is preserved while an error is surfaced
- prove unreachable local endpoints fail within a bounded timeout and include the attempted URL/base URL in the resulting error
- prove missing API keys for cloud configurations fail at call time, not provider construction time
- where practical, prove the documented default local OpenAI-compatible base URLs remain constructible without extra configuration

Relevant files: `provider/openai/openai_test.go`, `provider/anthropic/anthropic_test.go`, and supporting provider code.
title: Add provider edge-case tests for interrupted streams, endpoint errors, and call-time auth failures

Add the independent catalog publication path. The design requires an immutable versioned manifest bundle plus a stable channel pointer so operators and DDx can refresh policy faster than binary release cadence. This work should choose the actual static publishing mechanism, emit machine-readable metadata, and keep the source manifest reviewable in-repo.

The output must be easy for ddx-agent and DDx to fetch without scraping GitHub HTML or release pages.
title: Publish versioned shared model catalog bundles

acceptance: (1) reasoning_byte_limit and reasoning_stall_timeout are configurable in config.yaml (provider or global level). (2) Defaults raised to 256KB and 300s. (3) 0 means unlimited for both, matching max_iterations pattern. (4) Error messages include model name and current threshold value. (5) go test ./... passes; stream_consume_test.go covers configurable-limit paths.
description:
Hardcoded constants in stream_consume.go abort model responses prematurely for local models that use extended thinking:

- `reasoningByteLimit = 32 * 1024` (32KB) — aborts if pure reasoning_content exceeds 32K chars before any content delta
- `reasoningStallTimeout = 120 * time.Second` — aborts if only reasoning tokens arrive for 2 minutes

Observed failures:
- google/gemma-4-31b on LM Studio: reasoning overflow at 32KB on a small P3 bead
- qwen3.5-27b on LM Studio: reasoning stall on same bead
- Both models use extended thinking by default; coding tasks routinely produce >32KB of reasoning before the first content token
- Local models at 5-15 tok/s can easily spend 2+ minutes reasoning — 120s is normal behavior, not a stall
- qwen/qwen3-coder-next does NOT hit this (emits content tokens earlier)

## Fix scope

1. Make both thresholds configurable via config.yaml (reasoning_byte_limit, reasoning_stall_timeout)
2. Raise defaults: 256KB byte limit, 300s stall timeout
3. Allow 0 = unlimited for both (same pattern as max_iterations)
4. Include model name and current threshold in error messages

Relevant files: stream_consume.go (lines 13, 18), config/config.go.

GitHub issue: https://github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/agent/issues/1
resolution: fixed: reasoning_byte_limit and reasoning_stall_timeout configurable in config.yaml, defaults raised to 256KB/300s
title: fix: make reasoning stall/overflow thresholds configurable and raise defaults

acceptance: (1) GET /v1/models is enumerated by the discovery layer; the catalog is the canonical model-ID source per provider. (2) Bare model names ('qwen3-coder-next') passed by callers are normalized to canonical IDs ('qwen/qwen3-coder-next') by suffix-match against the catalog, with explicit ambiguity errors. (3) providers command shows http-connected and inference-ready-models per provider. (4) Routing policy consumes inference-ready-models, not 'connected (N models)'. (5) Regression test uses a mock LM Studio that lists 'qwen/foo' but rejects 'foo' on chat/completions; discovery normalizes 'foo' → 'qwen/foo' and the probe succeeds.
description:
Discovered 2026-04-15 while routing work to vidar-coder (LM Studio on vidar:1234).

## Two-part bug

### Part 1: bare-name JIT miss (the actual root cause)

Initial probe: `POST /v1/chat/completions {model: 'qwen3-coder-next', ...}` → 400 'No models loaded'.

We assumed LM Studio had no model loaded. Wrong. The actual model is listed by /v1/models as 'qwen/qwen3-coder-next' (with the 'qwen/' prefix). LM Studio's just-in-time loading IS enabled and DOES auto-load models on demand — but only when the requested model ID matches one in /v1/models exactly. Bare-name 'qwen3-coder-next' didn't match, so JIT couldn't fire, and LM Studio returned the misleading 'No models loaded' error instead of 'unknown model'.

Verified by re-probing with 'qwen/qwen3-coder-next' — JIT fired, model loaded, responded 'OK' in ~60s on cold start.

### Part 2: discovery should be standard practice

The routing/probe layer should never trust user-supplied bare model names against an LM Studio provider. The standard discovery flow should be:

1. GET /v1/models → enumerate the catalog the server is offering
2. For each catalog ID, optionally probe POST /v1/chat/completions with a 1-token request and a generous timeout (60s+ on cold start, 3s on warm)
3. Cache the (provider, model_id) → inference-ready bool with a short TTL
4. When user code asks for 'model qwen3-coder-next' against this provider, normalize against the discovered catalog: if there's exactly one match by suffix, accept it; if zero or multiple, error with the candidate list

This converts the 'No models loaded' misleading error into a clear 'requested model qwen3-coder-next not in catalog; closest match: qwen/qwen3-coder-next' (or just transparent normalization).

## Impact on cost-tiered routing

The cost-tiered routing policy (DDx bead ddx-dc231144 tier-based auto-escalation) needs reliable provider readiness signals. Without discovery + normalization:
- bare-name configs silently fail and force escalation
- the 'connected (N models)' status is unreliable as a routing input
- every misrouted attempt burns latency and forces the next tier

## Fix scope

- Update routing/health probe in cmd/ddx-agent/routing.go (or equivalent) to enumerate /v1/models and probe each listed model with its exact ID
- Add a model-id normalization layer that maps bare names to catalog IDs by suffix-match (with explicit error on ambiguity)
- Cache probe results per (provider, model) for a short TTL (30s reasonable)
- Surface the distinction in 'ddx-agent providers' output: http-connected (from /v1/models) vs inference-ready-models (the subset that actually responds)
resolution: fixed: /v1/models discovery + bare-name normalization in provider/openai
title: bug: /v1/models is not a liveness probe — providers shows 'connected' when LM Studio has no model loaded

Current state: discovery_test.go covers DiscoverModels, RankModels, NormalizeModelID, and lazy model discovery but has NO tests for:
- LookupModelLimits (all dispatch paths)
- probeProviderFlavor (omlx probe wins, lmstudio probe wins, both fail, flavor param bypasses probe)
- lmstudioLimits (loaded_context_length vs max_context_length priority)
- omlxLimits (model match by EqualFold)
- openrouterLimits (hyphen-dot normalization)

Required tests (httptest.NewServer mocks):
1. LookupModelLimits: explicit flavor='lmstudio' routes without probing
2. LookupModelLimits: explicit flavor='omlx' routes without probing
3. LookupModelLimits: explicit flavor='openrouter' routes without probing
4. LookupModelLimits: ambiguous URL fires probe; omlx probe wins
5. LookupModelLimits: ambiguous URL fires probe; lmstudio probe wins
6. LookupModelLimits: both probes fail → falls back to openAIIdentity result
7. LookupModelLimits: unreachable endpoint returns ModelLimits{} without error
8. lmstudioLimits: prefers loaded_context_length over max_context_length
9. omlxLimits: case-insensitive model match
10. openrouterLimits: hyphen-dot normalization (claude-haiku-4-5 == claude-haiku-4.5)
title: test: LookupModelLimits and probeProviderFlavor unit tests

acceptance: go test ./... passes; new test TestRun_MidTurnOverflowRetry exists in loop_test.go and covers: (1) provider returns context-overflow error on first call mid-turn, (2) compaction runs, (3) provider is called again with compacted history, (4) turn completes successfully with no ErrContextOverflow returned to caller
description:
<context-digest>
<principles>Design for Change · Design for Simplicity · Validate Your Work · Make Intent Explicit · Prefer Reversible Decisions</principles>
<concerns>go-std | testing</concerns>
<practices>gofmt · go vet · golangci-lint · gosec · govulncheck · go test · go test -race · testify/assert for assertions</practices>
<adrs>ADR-001 — OTel GenAI spans and JSONL session log are separate observability surfaces; error paths must record spans with error semantics</adrs>
<governing>SD-006 §Mid-iteration check — after tool results are appended, check context usage; if overflow, compact in-place and retry the same turn without aborting. FEAT-001 AC-FEAT-001-03 — transient retry and error paths must not issue extra provider calls beyond policy.</governing>
</context-digest>

Currently loop.go handles context overflow by compacting and then aborting the turn. Codex discovered that aborting is unnecessary — after compaction succeeds, the same provider call can be retried within the same turn.

What to change in loop.go:
- In the mid-turn overflow recovery path (after IsContextOverflowError is detected and compaction runs), instead of returning an error, retry the pending provider call with the compacted history.
- Cap mid-turn retries at 1 (if the compacted history still overflows, abort with error to avoid infinite retry).
- Emit EventCompactionStart / EventCompactionEnd as today; add a mid_turn: true field on the compaction events so callers can distinguish mid-turn from pre-turn compaction.
- All existing overflow/compaction tests must still pass.
- Add new test TestRun_MidTurnOverflowRetry.

Governing specs: SD-006, FEAT-001.
title: feat: mid-turn overflow compaction — retry turn after inline compact

acceptance: DDX Agent governing artifacts explicitly define the shared model catalog and external manifest requirement; prompt presets are clearly distinguished from model policy; ownership split across agent, DDx, and HELIX is documented; downstream beads agent-94b5d420 and agent-66eef6fe are traceable to the evolved artifacts.
description:
<context-digest>
<principles>Design for Change · Make Intent Explicit · Validate Your Work · Prefer Reversible Decisions</principles>
<concerns>go-std | testing</concerns>
<practices>go library packages should have clear ownership boundaries; rapidly changing policy/data should be separable from core code; design docs must reflect the actual ownership split with downstream consumers</practices>
<governing>Recent discussion established a new ownership model: agent should own the reusable shared model catalog and updateable manifest; DDx should consume that catalog for harness/model resolution and guardrails; HELIX should own stage intent only. Existing agent docs (notably FEAT-004 / SD-005) currently focus on provider/routing concepts and do not yet explicitly thread this shared-model-catalog requirement through the artifact stack.</governing>
<evidence>
- agent has prompt presets already (`prompt/presets.go`), so model policy must remain a separate concept and naming surface.
- DDx currently duplicates model policy in `cli/internal/agent/models.go` and `cli/internal/agent/registry.go`.
- HELIX currently duplicates concrete model-version policy in `scripts/helix`.
- New agent beads `agent-94b5d420` and `agent-66eef6fe` cover design/build work for a shared model catalog, but the governing agent artifacts have not yet been explicitly evolved to reflect that requirement.
- Model release cadence is high enough that agent likely needs a separately maintained manifest/data source rather than only hardcoded Go tables.
</evidence>
</context-digest>

Thread the new shared-model-catalog requirement through agent's governing artifacts before implementation.

Scope:
- Identify which agent artifacts must change to make the shared model catalog canonical (PRD, FEAT-004, SD-005, SD-003, and any adjacent artifact that defines prompt presets, routing, or config ownership).
- Update artifact language so prompt presets, model tiers/profiles, aliases, canonical targets, deprecations, and manifest/data ownership are clearly separated.
- Make the ownership split explicit: agent owns model catalog data/policy, DDx consumes it for harness orchestration, HELIX consumes DDx tier/model resolution for stage execution.
- Clarify how the separately maintained model manifest relates to agent releases and consumer update workflows.
- Ensure downstream design/build beads (`agent-94b5d420`, `agent-66eef6fe`) trace cleanly back to the updated governing artifacts.

Expected deliverables:
- Updated agent planning/design artifacts that explicitly authorize a shared model catalog + external manifest.
- Clear terminology that distinguishes prompt presets from model policy.
- Traceability from evolved artifacts to the existing agent design/build beads.
title: Evolve agent artifacts for shared model catalog and external manifest

acceptance: Session log analysis produces a clear characterization of the timeout cause (model capability ceiling vs. agent loop defect). If a loop defect is identified, a follow-on fix bead is filed with the specific loop condition to patch. cancel-async-tasks result in session log shows either: graceful give-up before timeout, or evidence of productive iteration toward solution.
description:
<context-digest>
<principles>Design for simplicity · Tests first</principles>
<concerns>benchmark | agent-loop</concerns>
<practices>Go · ddx-agent CLI · Harbor</practices>
<governing>SD-009 benchmark mode</governing>
</context-digest>

cancel-async-tasks times out in every ddx-agent run regardless of model:
- qwen3.5-27b: timeout in all 3 runs (t1/t2/t3), even with 2x timeout multiplier (30 min)
- claude-opus-4.6-fast: also FAILED this task in the 2026-04-10 evidence-grade baseline (8/15 run)

Task description: implement async run_tasks() with max concurrency and graceful SIGINT cleanup. Rated 'hard', expert time estimate 120-600 min. Agent timeout in task.toml is 900s.

The timeout may reflect either: (a) genuine model capability ceiling — the task requires subtle async/signal handling that current models get wrong and loop on; (b) ddx-agent loop issue where the agent keeps retrying a broken implementation without converging; (c) context overflow causing repeated compaction without progress.

Investigation steps:
1. Pull the session log from a timed-out cancel-async-tasks run (benchmark-results/harbor-jobs/cancel-async-tasks-*/sessions/*.jsonl) and analyze the loop pattern — is the agent making progress, looping on the same error, or hitting context overflow?
2. Check if the compaction/overflow recovery added in 20eba26 prevents runaway loops or makes them worse for this task type.
3. Determine if gpt-oss-120b or another model passes this task — if so, it's a model capability issue; if not, investigate loop termination.

Note: do NOT over-invest in making qwen3.5-27b pass this specific task. The primary value is understanding whether the agent loop is healthy (converges or gives up gracefully) vs. unhealthy (loops indefinitely on the same failure).

events.body: qwen3.5-27b generated 221,567 chars of pure reasoning tokens (48,206 llm.delta events) with zero content output and zero tool calls over 1800s. The model wrote the same Python code block 128 identical times in its reasoning chain but never committed to an answer or called any tool. Root cause: thinking-budget misconfiguration. The benchmark config.yaml uses type=openai-compat with model=qwen3.5-27b but sets no thinking_budget/thinking_level, so LM Studio serves the model with no reasoning cap. The picompat auto-configure path (which would auto-set thinking_level=medium for qwen3 models via isThinkingModel()) only runs when translating Claude Desktop config, not for direct config.yaml runs. loop.go is healthy: it made exactly one LLM call (iteration 0 of max_iterations=20) and the timeout hit at the provider level (Harbor asyncio.CancelledError) while awaiting the first llm.response that never arrived. No loop defect found; no follow-on fix bead warranted.
events.summary: Model runaway reasoning, not a loop defect

title: investigate: cancel-async-tasks timeout — loop analysis and model capability floor

acceptance: Unit test constructs an agent loop scenario where the mock provider returns the same tool call 4 times in a row and asserts the loop exits with ErrToolCallLoop after 3 identical turns. A second test asserts the counter resets when a different tool call appears mid-sequence. Loop exits with StatusError and code 0 in benchmark mode.
description:
The agent loop has no detection for iteration-level loops where the model repeatedly calls the same tool with the same arguments without making progress. Observed pattern: agent calls bash('go test ./...'), gets a compile error, calls bash('go test ./...') again unchanged, same error, repeats up to max_iterations.

Implementation: after each tool call round-trip, hash (tool_name, normalized_tool_args) for each call in the turn. Track consecutive turns where the full set of tool calls is identical to the previous turn. After N identical consecutive turns (default 3), abort the loop with a sentinel error (ErrToolCallLoop or similar) and exit non-retryably.

Reset the counter when any turn produces a different tool call set.

Files: loop.go (tool call dispatch and iteration), errors.go (sentinel). The hash/comparison should be cheap — a simple string concat or encoding/json marshal of the call list is fine.
title: fix: detect and abort identical consecutive tool-call loops

acceptance: Running ddx-agent --json --preset benchmark on a task that produces correct output exits with code 0. Harbor no longer records NonZeroAgentExitCodeError for solved tasks. Verified by re-running build-pmars and code-from-image tasks and observing n_errors=0 in result.json alongside score=1.0.
description:
<context-digest>
<principles>Tests first · Design for simplicity</principles>
<concerns>benchmark | harness</concerns>
<practices>Go · ddx-agent CLI</practices>
<governing>SD-009 benchmark mode</governing>
</context-digest>

When ddx-agent completes a task successfully (Harbor verifier scores it 1.0), the process still exits with a non-zero exit code. Harbor records this as NonZeroAgentExitCodeError and counts it as an error in n_errors, even though the reward is 1.0.

Observed in benchmark run 20260412T172048Z:
- build-pmars: score=1.0, NonZeroAgentExitCodeError
- code-from-image: score=1.0, NonZeroAgentExitCodeError

Root cause to investigate: the agent likely exits non-zero when the session ends with no more iterations (ErrNoMoreAttempts or similar) or when the --json flag causes the process to exit with a status reflecting internal state rather than task outcome. In benchmark mode the exit code should be 0 on clean completion regardless of whether the model hit iteration limits.

Files to check: cmd/ddx-agent/main.go exit path, loop.go termination conditions. The fix is likely setting exit code 0 when the agent loop ends cleanly (exhausted iterations or received done signal) vs. exits on an unrecoverable error.
title: fix: agent exits non-zero after successful task completion in benchmark harness

acceptance: (1) When a model has context_window set in models.yaml, the compaction config uses that value instead of DefaultContextWindow. (2) The CLI main.go and embedded-agent paths both resolve context_window from the catalog when LookupModelLimits returns 0. (3) LM Studio models with no /v1/models context_length field get the catalog value, not the default. (4) go test ./compaction ./modelcatalog ./cmd/ddx-agent -count=1 passes with at least one test verifying catalog-driven context window flows to compaction config.
description:
## Problem

When LM Studio does not expose context_length in /v1/models (it only returns {id, object, owned_by}), the agent falls back to DefaultContextWindow. Before v0.3.9 that was 8192; now it is 131072. But models like qwen3-coder-next and qwen3.5-27b have 262144 — the compaction fires at 124k (95% of 131k) instead of ~249k.

The v4 catalog manifest already has context_window per model (added in v0.3.9 for qwen models). The standalone CLI (cmd/ddx-agent/main.go) does use LookupModelLimits which queries the API, but when that returns 0 it should fall back to the catalog entry rather than the hardcoded default.

## Fix

1. In cmd/ddx-agent/main.go after LookupModelLimits returns 0, look up the model in the catalog and use its context_window.
2. Export a helper (e.g. modelcatalog.ContextWindowForModel) that the DDx embedded runner can also call.
3. DDx will consume the helper in embeddedCompactionConfig() to get accurate per-model windows.

## Files to touch
- modelcatalog/catalog.go — add ContextWindowForModel(modelID string) int helper
- cmd/ddx-agent/main.go — fall back to catalog context_window when API returns 0
- compaction/config.go — no changes (default is already 131k)
- modelcatalog/catalog_test.go — test ContextWindowForModel returns correct value for qwen3.5-27b

## Review linkage
Discovered / re-confirmed in AR-2026-04-17 alignment pass (agent-bad5ec4e). CLI currently falls through from LookupModelLimits→0 directly to DefaultContextWindow=131072 without checking catalog.
title: Wire per-model context_window from v4 catalog into compaction config

acceptance: Prompt construction test confirms stable prefix is byte-identical across repeated calls with same context; no timestamps or dynamic fields in system prompt block
description:
Cache efficiency requires that stable content (system prompt, persona, tool definitions, file context) comes before variable content (user message, current task). Any change to early tokens invalidates all downstream cache entries.

Audit prompt construction in ddx-agent and enforce ordering rules:
1. System prompt / persona — must be deterministic, no dynamic fields (timestamps, file lists, git hashes in this block)
2. Tool definitions — stable, comes after system prompt
3. Persistent file context — ordered consistently (e.g. alphabetically) within a session
4. Conversation history — append-only, never reorder
5. Current user turn — always last

Add a test that verifies the same session produces byte-identical prefix across multiple calls.
title: Enforce stable prefix ordering in prompt construction

acceptance: go test ./... passes; loop_test covers overflow→compact→retry path; overflow without compactor returns error immediately; double-overflow after compaction returns error without looping; go vet ./... clean
description:
<context-digest>
<principles>Design for simplicity · No speculative abstractions</principles>
<concerns>go-std | testing</concerns>
<practices>Go stdlib · testify/assert</practices>
<governing>SD-001 §agent loop; SD-006 §compaction</governing>
</context-digest>

When a provider call fails with a context overflow error, automatically trigger compaction and retry the failed turn rather than returning ErrCompactionNoFit to the caller.

**Problem**: When context overflows mid-session, ddx-agent returns `ErrCompactionNoFit` and stops. Pi detects the overflow error, removes it from agent state, triggers compaction, and retries the turn transparently. Long-running tasks on small-context local models just die in ddx-agent.

**Reference implementation**: pi `agent-session.ts` lines 1765-1788 — detects overflow in the LLM error response, removes the overflow message, invokes compaction, then re-queues the turn.

**Files to change**:
- `loop.go` — in the provider call error path, detect context overflow errors (pattern match on "context length", "token limit", "maximum context", "context window") and if a Compactor is configured, invoke it and retry the current turn once
- `compaction/` — verify `ErrCompactionNoFit` is propagated correctly so the retry can distinguish "compaction helped" from "still too large"

**Behaviour**:
- On overflow: attempt compaction → retry turn once
- If compaction fails or post-compaction turn still overflows: return error (no infinite loop)
- Only triggers when a Compactor is configured; otherwise behaves as before
title: Overflow-triggered auto-compaction and retry

acceptance: An environment-gated integration test covers AC-FEAT-002-06 by exercising the benchmark-oriented tool surface against a real provider, asserting on tool-call logs and workspace mutations; the integration suite passes when a backend is configured and otherwise skips explicitly.

The current integration suite proves simple read/edit flows against LM Studio, but it does not exercise the broader shipped tool surface that FEAT-002 now claims. Add at least one environment-gated model-backed acceptance scenario that drives a real provider through navigation and editing work using the benchmark-oriented tools (`glob`, `grep`, `ls`, `patch`, and/or `task`) rather than only `read`/`edit`.

The test should:
- create a disposable workspace with a small but non-trivial file graph
- prompt the model to discover a target via navigation tools, make a change, and confirm the result
- assert on actual tool-call logs and resulting files, not just the final assistant text
- skip explicitly when the configured integration environment is unavailable

Relevant files: `integration_test.go`, `tool/`, and any provider-specific integration helpers.

acceptance: picompat/auth.go, picompat/models.go, picompat/settings.go, picompat/translate.go implemented; unit tests compile
description:
Implement pi config readers per SD-007 tasks 1-3.
Required files:
- picompat/auth.go: reads ~/.pi/agent/auth.json → map[provider]credential
- picompat/models.go: reads ~/.pi/agent/models.json → map[provider]providerDef
- picompat/settings.go: reads ~/.pi/agent/settings.json → default provider/model
- picompat/translate.go: merges auth+models+settings → []config.ProviderConfig

Pi provider name → agent mapping per SD-007:
- anthropic → anthropic (type: anthropic)
- openai-codex → openai (type: openai-compat, default URL)
- openrouter → openrouter (type: openai-compat)
- Custom from models.json → mapped from api field

Two-source merge algorithm per SD-007:
1. Start with models.json providers (have baseUrl and model IDs)
2. Look up matching auth.json credentials
3. For auth-only entries, use well-known defaults
4. Apply settings.json defaultProvider/defaultModel

Skipped with warnings per SD-007:
- !command API key values
- Unsupported api field values
- headers with !command resolution

Output uses SD-005 field names exactly.
notes: Implemented: auth.json reader, models.json reader, settings.json reader, translate.go with two-source merge, known mappings, warnings, hash computation
title: Implement picompat package (pi config readers)

acceptance: DDX Agent design docs define a shared model catalog and separately maintained model manifest; ownership split between agent, DDx, and HELIX is explicit; aliases, tiers/profiles, canonical targets, and stale-model metadata are specified; the design explains how downstream tools consume the catalog without duplicating model policy.
description:
<context-digest>
<principles>Design for Change · Make Intent Explicit · Validate Your Work · Prefer Reversible Decisions</principles>
<concerns>go-std | testing</concerns>
<practices>small reusable library packages in Go · explicit config/data boundaries · tests beside implementation</practices>
<governing>Recent FEAT-004 / SD-005 routing work exposed that agent already owns prompt presets, but no shared model catalog exists. The user wants agent, not DDx or HELIX, to be the reusable source of truth for canonical model families, aliases, current recommended targets, and deprecation metadata.</governing>
<evidence>
- prompt/presets.go already uses the term `preset` for system prompt behavior, so model policy must avoid colliding terminology.
- DDx currently duplicates model policy in cli/internal/agent/models.go and cli/internal/agent/registry.go.
- HELIX currently duplicates concrete model version strings in scripts/helix resolve_model().
- Model releases change rapidly, so keeping canonical model/version mappings only in Go code is too rigid.
- The desired direction is a agent-owned library package plus a separately maintained model manifest in the agent repo that downstream tools can consume.
</evidence>
</context-digest>

Design a agent-owned shared model catalog and updateable model manifest.

Scope:
- Define the package/API shape for a reusable agent model catalog that DDx and agent CLI can consume.
- Define the manifest format, storage location, and update workflow so model/version data can be maintained separately from Go logic.
- Define how the catalog represents aliases, model families, tiers/profiles (e.g. smart / fast / cheap), canonical current targets, and deprecated/stale model versions.
- Define how non-agent harness families (e.g. Claude Code / Codex) can still consume agent-owned catalog data without pushing harness orchestration into agent runtime.
- Clarify the responsibility split: agent owns catalog data/policy, DDx owns harness orchestration, HELIX owns stage intent.
- Identify how FEAT-004 / SD-005 and any neighboring agent artifacts should evolve to reference this shared catalog.

Expected deliverables:
- Governing agent docs updated with the shared model catalog concept.
- A design for data-vs-code separation so the model list can be updated independently of core code releases where practical.
- Consumer examples for DDx and agent CLI.
title: Design agent shared model catalog and updateable model manifest

Extend the thinking budget from a raw token integer to named levels (off/low/medium/high) mapped to token budgets, configurable per-provider.

**Problem**: ddx-agent currently has a single static `ThinkingBudget int` set at provider construction time. Pi supports named levels with per-model budget mappings and allows dynamic switching. For local models, the right budget depends on task complexity; a single hardcoded value is too coarse.

**Files to change**:
- `agent.go` — add `ThinkingLevel` type (string enum: off/low/medium/high) to `Options`; keep `ThinkingBudget int` as the resolved token count
- `config/config.go` `ProviderConfig` — add `ThinkingLevel string` field; if set, resolve to token budget using defaults table; `ThinkingBudget int` remains for explicit override
- `provider/openai/openai.go` — accept level or budget; level "off" sends `thinking: {type: "disabled"}` if supported, otherwise omits param
- Default budget mapping: off=0, low=2048, medium=8192, high=32768

**Behaviour**:
- `thinking_level: low` in config.yaml → resolved to 2048 tokens
- `thinking_level: off` → no thinking param sent
- Explicit `thinking_budget: N` overrides level
- Both remain per-provider in config.yaml (not global)
title: Dynamic thinking budget levels (off/low/medium/high)

description:

Cap tool output size before sending to the LLM to prevent context explosion on local models with small context windows.

**Problem**: ddx-agent currently sends raw untruncated tool output to the LLM. A bash command dumping 500KB floods the context window, causing overflow errors or degraded performance on local models.

**Reference implementation**: pi uses `truncateHead()` for file reads (keep first N lines) and `truncateTail()` for bash output (keep last N lines, preserving errors). Emits `[Truncated: X lines omitted]` marker.

**Files to change**:
- `tools/bash.go` (or equivalent tool executor) — truncate stdout/stderr
- `tools/read.go` — truncate file content  
- `tools/grep.go` — truncate grep output
- Add shared `truncate.go` utility in `tools/` package

**Limits** (match pi):
- Max 2000 lines OR 50KB per tool output, whichever hits first
- bash/grep: keep tail (errors are at end)
- read: keep head (file start is most relevant)
- Append `\n[Truncated: N lines omitted]` when truncated
title: Tool output truncation — cap outputs before LLM send

acceptance: Route resolution works from requested model, model_ref, and routing defaults; priority-round-robin and ordered-failover are deterministic; passive cooldown state affects selection without synchronous probe requirements; failover classification is explicit and tested; aggregated routing errors include the attempt chain when every candidate fails.
description: Replace backend-pool resolution in the runtime-facing config/CLI path with model-route resolution keyed by requested model or resolved canonical target. Implement deterministic candidate selection for priority-round-robin and ordered-failover. Add passive health state so recently failed candidates can be skipped for a cooldown window, but do not block on active probes before every request. Failover must advance only on provider-side availability failures such as transport errors, auth failures, upstream 429/5xx, or unreachable endpoints; prompt-shape, tool-schema, and other deterministic request errors must not fan out across candidates. Persist only the minimal route state needed for repeatable rotation and cooldowns. Background: the user wants to say ddx-agent run --model qwen3.5-27b and have the runtime choose among bragi/vidar/grendel/openrouter based on actual availability, while DDx remains unaware of inner provider candidates.
title: Implement model-route resolver, passive health state, and failover classification

acceptance: models.yaml at schema v4 with per-model entries for all current active targets; manifest.go parses models section; catalog.go exposes AllModelsInTier and LookupModel; PricingFor iterates model entries; UpdateManifestPricing updates per-model costs; v3 manifests load without error via compat upgrade path; go test ./modelcatalog/... covers all new API surfaces.
description:
The current manifest (v3) treats each policy tier (code-high/medium/economy) as the model itself: one cost, one benchmark score, one model per surface. This blocks multi-model tier routing.

Proposed manifest v4 changes:

1. Add a top-level `models` section. Each entry is a concrete model (e.g., qwen3.5-27b, qwen3.5-7b, claude-haiku-5.5) with:
   - family, display_name, tier (reference to parent target), status
   - cost_input_per_m, cost_output_per_m, cost_cache_read_per_m, cost_cache_write_per_m
   - context_window, swe_bench_verified (nullable), live_code_bench (nullable), benchmark_as_of
   - openrouter_id (exact OR model ID)
   - surfaces: map of surface → concrete model ID string

2. Extend target entries with a `candidates` list referencing model IDs from the models section. The ordering is the default capability rank within the tier.

3. Remove cost and openrouter_ref fields from targets (they move to individual model entries). Targets retain: family, aliases, status, context_window_min (floor for tier qualification), swe_bench_min, surface_policy.

4. Update manifest.go to parse the new schema. Update catalog.go to expose per-model data via AllModelsInTier(targetID) and LookupModel(modelID) APIs. Update PricingFor() to iterate model entries not target surfaces.

5. Update UpdateManifestPricing() in openrouter.go to match per-model openrouter_id entries and update per-model costs.

6. Update models.yaml with initial per-model entries for claude-haiku-5.5, qwen3.5-27b, qwen3.5-7b (code-economy candidates), gpt-5.4-mini / sonnet-4.6 (code-medium), gpt-5.4 / opus-4.6 (code-high).

7. Bump manifest schema version to 4. Add backward-compat reader that upgrades v3 manifests at load time (synthesizes model entries from target surfaces).

Governing: FEAT-004, SD-005. Update both specs to reflect v4 shape.
title: design: manifest v4 schema — per-model entries, tier candidates, and multi-model tiers

acceptance: Unit test feeds a stream of pure reasoning_content deltas past the threshold and asserts the stream read returns the sentinel error. The agent loop treats the error as non-retryable and exits cleanly (exit code 0 in benchmark mode). Both the byte-threshold and stall-timer paths are exercised.
description:
When a thinking model generates reasoning tokens without ever producing output content or tool calls, the agent hangs until the external timeout (Harbor's asyncio.wait_for) fires. Observed in cancel-async-tasks: qwen3.5-27b emitted 221k reasoning chars over 1800s, rewrote the same solution 128 times, and never finished.

The agent loop should detect this condition and abort the stream early rather than waiting for the provider or harness to kill it.

Detection heuristics (implement at least one, preferably both):
1. Reasoning-token threshold: if accumulated reasoning content exceeds a configurable limit (default ~32k chars) with no content or tool_call delta yet seen, cancel the stream and return a sentinel error (ErrReasoningOverflow or similar).
2. Reasoning-stall timer: if only reasoning_content deltas arrive for longer than a configurable duration (default 120s) with no content or tool_call delta, cancel the stream.

The agent loop should treat ErrReasoningOverflow as a non-retryable loop-termination condition — log a warning and stop iterating rather than retrying.

Files likely involved: stream.go or stream_consume.go (stream reading), loop.go (error handling).
title: fix: detect runaway reasoning loop and abort stream early

acceptance: go test ./... passes; CompactionConfig gains UserMessageTailTokens field (default 20000); new test TestCompact_UserMessageTailReInclusion exists and verifies that after compaction the replacement history contains both the summary message and the most recent real user messages up to the token budget, in chronological order
description:
<context-digest>
<principles>Design for Change · Design for Simplicity · Validate Your Work · Make Intent Explicit · Prefer Reversible Decisions</principles>
<concerns>go-std | testing</concerns>
<practices>gofmt · go vet · golangci-lint · gosec · govulncheck · go test · go test -race · testify/assert for assertions</practices>
<adrs>ADR-001 — OTel GenAI spans and JSONL session log are separate observability surfaces</adrs>
<governing>SD-006 §Summary Injection — the compaction summary replaces all compacted messages. SD-006 §Codex research — keeps up to 20K tokens of recent user messages alongside summary. FEAT-001 AC-FEAT-001-06 — compaction no-fit paths must fail closed.</governing>
</context-digest>

Currently compaction/compaction.go replaces compacted history with only the summary message. Codex retains the most recent real user messages (up to a token budget) alongside the summary so the model retains actual request history.

What to change in compaction/compaction.go (or the Compactor function):
- After generating the summary, collect real user-role messages from the tail of the pre-compaction history (exclude system prefix messages, previous compaction summaries, and tool result messages — only real user messages).
- Accumulate messages from newest to oldest until a configurable token budget is exhausted (default: 20000 tokens, chars/4 heuristic). Per SD-006 §Overlong individual user messages, truncate individual messages that exceed the budget rather than dropping them.
- The replacement history becomes: [summary_message, ...tail_user_messages] ordered so the summary is first and user messages follow in chronological order.
- Expose the budget as a new field UserMessageTailTokens int in CompactionConfig (default 20000; 0 = disabled).
- All existing compaction tests must still pass.
- Add test TestCompact_UserMessageTailReInclusion verifying tail messages appear in replacement history.

Governing specs: SD-006, FEAT-001.
title: feat: compaction user-message tail re-inclusion after compact

acceptance:
1. `ddx-agent import pi` accepts the current Pi `models.json` shape where `providers` is an object map keyed by provider name.
2. Backward compatibility with the older array-based `providers` shape is preserved or explicitly rejected with a deterministic migration error if true compatibility is not feasible.
3. Import continues to translate Pi auth, provider URLs, model IDs, and default provider/model settings into valid `ddx-agent` config.
4. Regression tests cover both Pi schema variants and prove `import pi --diff` and normal import behavior work against realistic fixtures.
description:
`ddx-agent import pi` is stale against the current Pi config layout. Today the importer fails with:

`reading pi models.json: json: cannot unmarshal object into Go struct field ModelsConfig.providers of type []picompat.ProviderDefinition`

Current Pi stores `~/.pi/agent/models.json` with `providers` as an object map keyed by provider name, while `picompat/models.go` still expects the older array form. That makes the import surface unusable for real Pi installs even though the command still advertises `pi` as a supported source.

Update `picompat` and the import CLI to accept the current Pi schema while preserving compatibility with the older format if it still exists in the field. The importer should continue translating provider auth, base URLs, models, and defaults into `ddx-agent` config without requiring manual edits.
title: Fix pi import for current models.json schema

acceptance: (1) Compaction loop bounded with a structural stop. (2) Native structured error signals stuck state to callers. (3) Regression test reproduces the stuck state via seeded read-only tool calls. (4) DDx repo can remove the caller-side circuit breaker once this upstream fix is consumed.
description:
The compaction package can enter a stuck state where compaction.end events fire with no_compaction=true and success=false repeatedly, pulsing the inactivity timer on every iteration so the caller's timeout never fires. Downstream consumer DDx ran into this mid-session on a production worker: ~8461 stuck-compaction events accumulated before the hosting process had to be killed.

## Evidence

DDx-side session log:
/Users/erik/Projects/axon/.ddx/executions/20260415T005652-ebe7a16a/embedded/agent-1776214612561376672.jsonl (33 MB)
Parent process killed at PID 4141811 after wedging for hours on a completed task.

## DDx-side workaround (defense in depth, does not fix the root cause)

Commit 604151773f9b905346ee95b051e024a02f850cf0 in ddx repo (bead ddx-25a41f51) wraps the embedded agent call with two guards:
1. compaction.start and compaction.end(no_compaction=true) events no longer pulse the caller's inactivity timer — only real-progress events (LLM calls, tool results, commits) extend the timeout.
2. Circuit breaker counts consecutive compaction.end(success=false, no_compaction=true) events and aborts with a structured compaction_stuck error after a configurable threshold (default 50).

The workaround catches the stuck state at the DDx/caller layer. The native fix belongs in github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/agent so every ddx-agent user (not just DDx CLI) benefits.

## Requested upstream fix

- Compaction driver detects its own stuck state via the same heuristic (consecutive success=false/no_compaction=true events) and returns a structured error to the caller
- Eliminates the need for the caller to interpret compaction events as activity signals
- Exposes a clean API signal so consumers can decide whether to retry, escalate, or abort
- Regression test seeds the stuck state and verifies the driver bails instead of looping

## Scope

- Bounded attempt counter inside the compaction loop (not just the caller)
- Structured error: compaction.ErrStuck or similar
- Integration test using a deterministic seed that reproduces the 33MB jsonl pattern

## Relationship to DDx

When this lands upstream, DDx can remove its circuit-breaker workaround and rely on the native error. Until then, DDx keeps both layers as belt-and-suspenders.
resolution: fixed: bounded compaction loop with ErrCompactionStuck sentinel
title: bug: compaction loop can enter stuck state — no_compaction=true repeating without bound

description: Terminal-Bench is relevant to ddx-agent because the harness evaluates autonomous coding agents in a real terminal sandbox, and the current Terminal-Bench source supports installed-agent adapters that can invoke a CLI inside the task container. ForgeCode's public benchmark writeup also shows that benchmark gains come from harness/runtime engineering, not model choice alone; we should measure ddx-agent independently instead of inferring quality from anecdote or vendor claims.
notes: Amended per Claude review on 2026-04-08: benchmark credibility requires an explicit access/interface audit, a committed fixed task subset, and a measured baseline before downstream thresholds or tool-addition beads are treated as meaningful.
title: Benchmark ddx-agent on Terminal-Bench and close benchmark-critical harness gaps

description:
ddx is maintaining a parallel model catalog with pricing (cost_input_per_m, cost_output_per_m, cost_cache_read_per_m, cost_cache_write_per_m), benchmark scores (swe_bench_verified, live_code_bench, benchmark_as_of), and context window size. This data belongs in ddx-agent's catalog, not ddx.

Changes:
1. Extend targetEntry in modelcatalog/manifest.go with optional fields:
   cost_input_per_m, cost_output_per_m, cost_cache_read_per_m, cost_cache_write_per_m (float64 USD/1M tokens), context_window (int), swe_bench_verified, live_code_bench (float64 percent), benchmark_as_of (string YYYY-MM-DD), openrouter_ref_id (string)
2. Extend ResolvedTarget in modelcatalog/catalog.go to expose these fields
3. Populate models.yaml with current data:
   code-high: anthropic direct $15/$75 input/output, OR $5/$25; OpenAI OR $2.50/$15; swe_bench 80.8%; context 1M
   code-medium: anthropic $3/$15, haiku-cache $3.75/$0.30; openai $0.75/$4.50; swe_bench 79.6%; context 1M/400k
   code-economy: anthropic $0.80/$4 direct, openai $0.20/$1.56; swe_bench 73.3%; context 200k/262k
   openrouter_ref_id for anthropic targets: anthropic/claude-opus-4.6, anthropic/claude-sonnet-4.6, anthropic/claude-haiku-4.5
4. Add validation: cost fields >= 0 if present; context_window > 0 if present
5. Bump schema version to 3
title: Add pricing, benchmarks, and context metadata to catalog manifest

acceptance: Running helix run --agent codex --summary --max-cycles 1 in a repo with ready work dispatches codex rather than claude; the generated .helix-logs/helix-*.log for that run does not report harness=claude for implementation or automatic review steps; helix status and .ddx/run-state.json record the completed run agent as codex.
description:
<context-digest>
<principles>Make Intent Explicit · Prefer Deterministic Automation · Validate Your Work</principles>
<concerns>shell-workflows | agent-dispatch | run-state</concerns>
<practices>CLI flags must control the selected harness deterministically; persisted run metadata must match the requested agent; worker summary logs should be trustworthy for debugging</practices>
<governing>User explicitly launched HELIX with --agent codex from the agent repo. The selected harness and persisted run metadata should reflect that choice for both implementation and automatic review steps.</governing>
<evidence>
- Repro command run in /Users/erik/Projects/agent: helix run --agent codex --summary --max-cycles 1
- Generated log /Users/erik/Projects/agent/.helix-logs/helix-20260408-210317.log records implementation and review as harness=claude, not codex.

- .ddx/run-state.json after completion stores "agent": "claude".

acceptance: go test ./... passes; isTransientError correctly classifies socket hang up, 429, 503, connection refused as transient and auth errors as fatal; loop retries transient errors with backoff and fails fast on fatal errors; go vet ./... clean
description:
<context-digest>
<principles>Design for simplicity · No speculative abstractions</principles>
<concerns>go-std | testing</concerns>
<practices>Go stdlib · testify/assert</practices>
<governing>SD-001 §agent loop — provider retry; SD-004 §streaming; SD-005 §provider config</governing>
</context-digest>

Replace the generic 3-attempt retry in the agent loop with pattern-matched transient error detection and proper exponential backoff.

**Problem**: ddx-agent treats all provider errors identically. Local LLM servers (LMStudio, Ollama) commonly produce transient errors — socket hangs, connection resets, brief server restarts — that should be retried. Currently these look identical to fatal errors and the agent stops.

**Reference implementation**: pi matches errors against a large regex covering: `overloaded|rate.*limit|too many requests|429|500|502|503|504|service.*unavailable|server.*error|internal.*error|network.*error|connection.*error|connection.*refused|other side closed|fetch failed|socket hang up|ended without|timed? out|timeout|terminated`

**Files to change**:
- `loop.go` — replace current retry block (around line 377) with pattern-matched retry
- Add `provider_error.go` (or inline in loop.go) with `isTransientError(err error) bool` using regexp

**Behaviour**:
- Transient errors: retry up to 5 times with exponential backoff (1s, 2s, 4s, 8s, 16s)
- Fatal errors (auth, bad request, model not found): fail immediately, no retry
- Log retry attempt and reason at each backoff
title: Transient error retry patterns for provider calls

acceptance: Tests covering AC-FEAT-001-03 and AC-FEAT-001-06 exist in the runtime test suite; they prove retry exhaustion stops at the runtime retry ceiling, concurrent runs keep independent state, and the default Go test suite passes.
description:
<context-digest>
<principles>Runtime behavior should be proven at the `Run()` boundary · Regression tests should encode edge-case termination behavior, not just happy paths · Concurrent safety claims need explicit tests</principles>
<concerns>loop lifecycle | retries | cancellation | compaction fail-closed</concerns>
<practices>Add focused tests in `loop_test.go` or a companion runtime acceptance file · Use mock providers/tools with explicit call counters and controllable blocking points · Assert both result state and absence of extra provider calls</practices>
<adrs>ADR-001 for cost/telemetry semantics</adrs>
<governing>FEAT-001 AC-FEAT-001-03 and AC-FEAT-001-06</governing>
</context-digest>

Current loop coverage proves success, simple retries, cancellation before start, and compaction no-fit handling, but it does not explicitly cover retry exhaustion stopping at the configured runtime retry ceiling or concurrent `Run()` isolation. Add deterministic tests that:
- force repeated transient provider failures and prove the runtime stops after the documented retry budget without issuing a fourth provider call
- run multiple `Run()` calls concurrently with separate providers/tools/callbacks and prove session IDs, event streams, token totals, and tool logs do not leak across runs
- if needed to support the above, add a long-running mock tool path that can be cancelled so the runtime's documented cancellation semantics remain encoded at the boundary

Relevant files: `loop.go`, `loop_test.go`, `loop_external_test.go`.
title: Add loop lifecycle acceptance tests for retry exhaustion and concurrent isolation

acceptance: Drift detection notice on changed source files; daily debounce works
description:
Implement drift detection per SD-007 Drift Detection section.

Hash computation:
- Concatenate source files content (auth.json + models.json)
- Compute SHA-256, truncate to 8 hex chars
- Store in SourceHash field

Check logic per SD-007:
- On ddx-agent providers or ddx-agent -p, check if source files changed
- If imported_from exists and hash differs, emit once per day:
  "agent: pi config changed since import — run ddx-agent import pi --diff to review"
- Debounce via mtime of ~/.config/agent/.import-check-{source}
- Per-source tracking (pi and opencode tracked independently)

Token expiry warnings per SD-007:
- If OAuth token expires within 24h, warn with hours remaining
- If already expired, warn but still import
notes: Implemented checkDrift with hash comparison and daily debounce via .import-check-* files
title: Implement drift detection

acceptance: Replaying a historical session log whose session.end data contains `cost_usd` = -1 renders 'Cost: unknown'; replay still renders known positive costs and explicit zero-cost local runs correctly; regression coverage includes a legacy log fixture or equivalent hand-written JSONL input.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: session/replay.go:108
Category: integration
Severity: medium
Description: The new pointer-based `SessionEndData.CostUSD` handling only treats `nil` as unknown. Historical `session.end` logs produced before this commit encoded unknown cost as `cost_usd: -1`. When those logs are replayed now, `Replay` falls through the non-positive branch and renders `Cost: $0 (local)` instead of `Cost: unknown`, which misclassifies existing session history and breaks the forward-compatibility expectation for replaying older JSONL logs.
Suggested fix: Preserve compatibility for historical session logs by treating negative `cost_usd` values as unknown during replay (and ideally add regression coverage with a hand-written legacy log fixture containing `cost_usd: -1`).
title: integration: preserve legacy unknown-cost replay semantics

acceptance: When Run executes with a StreamingProvider and NoStream=false, the successful llm.response event includes attempt metadata with provider_name, provider_system, requested_model, response_model/resolved_model, and cost source; streaming and non-streaming paths both pass regression tests covering metadata propagation.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review of commit de8c772.
File: loop.go:162, loop.go:210, stream_consume.go:27, provider/openai/openai.go:184, provider/anthropic/anthropic.go:180
Category: correctness
Severity: high
Description: The new retry and attempt metadata only survives the non-streaming path. Run still prefers consumeStream whenever a provider implements StreamingProvider, which both OpenAI and Anthropic do. consumeStream assembles only content, tool calls, usage, model, and finish reason; it has no way to carry AttemptMetadata. The loop then backfills only AttemptIndex when resp.Attempt is nil. As a result, the default execution path loses ProviderName, ProviderSystem, RequestedModel, ResponseModel, ResolvedModel, and cost provenance even though this commit's acceptance requires runtime attempt metadata sufficient for provider identity and attribution. Current tests cover only non-streaming metadata population, so this regression is not caught.
Suggested fix: Extend the streaming path so provider metadata is emitted and preserved through StreamDelta and consumeStream, and add regression tests that run Run with streaming-enabled providers and assert the emitted llm.response attempt payload includes provider, model, and cost metadata on success.
title: correctness: streaming runs drop attempt metadata required by runtime retries

acceptance: Streaming timing uses real delta arrival timestamps rather than subtracting cumulative callback time; a regression test with callback sleep plus inter-delta provider delay verifies ddx.timing.generation_ms still includes the provider delay and EventLLMDelta timestamps remain monotonic.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: stream_consume.go:44
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: consumeStream now computes each delta timestamp as time.Now().Add(-callbackDelay) and grows callbackDelay by the duration of prior EventLLMDelta callbacks. When a later streamed delta becomes ready while an earlier callback is still running, this subtracts both observer latency and real provider generation time that elapsed during that callback. In the shipped providers, ChatStream uses a single-slot channel, so later deltas can queue behind the consumer and then be back-dated when read. That makes ddx.timing.generation_ms and delta event timestamps too small or even flat across distinct arrivals, which violates CONTRACT-001's requirement that timing reflect the streamed output window rather than an adjusted observer schedule.
Suggested fix: Capture an arrival timestamp at the producer or carry it on StreamDelta so consumeStream can use actual delta arrival times. At minimum, add a regression test that combines callback sleep with real delayBetween timing and verifies generation_ms still includes the provider delay that elapsed while the callback was running.
title: correctness: callback-delay compensation undercounts real stream generation

acceptance: HOME set to a fresh temp directory and running 'go run ./cmd/ddx-agent update --check-only' no longer fails because ~/.cache/agent is missing; cmd/ddx-agent has a regression test covering GetLatestRelease or cmdUpdate with an absent cache directory
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: cmd/ddx-agent/update.go:138
Category: integration
Severity: medium
Description: GetLatestRelease now returns any cache-write error from saveCachedVersion. cmdUpdate and cmdVersion pass ~/.cache/agent/latest-version.json without creating ~/.cache/agent first, so a first-run update check fails after a successful GitHub response with 'open .../.cache/agent/latest-version.json: no such file or directory'. Reproduced with: HOME=/tmp/tmp.guOtfw5Bpt go run ./cmd/ddx-agent update --check-only
Suggested fix: Treat cache persistence as best-effort or create the parent cache directory before writing, and add a regression test that covers a missing cache directory.
title: integration: update check fails on first run when cache directory is absent

acceptance: For Anthropic streaming runs, token usage from message_start and message_delta is merged into the final Response and llm.response event; Input, Output, CacheRead, and CacheWrite are preserved; Total equals Input+Output; a regression test covers split usage deltas
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review of commit 51f778b.
File: provider/anthropic/anthropic.go:236, provider/anthropic/anthropic.go:275, stream_consume.go:84
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: Anthropic streaming emits input tokens on the initial message_start delta and output/cache tokens on later message_delta events, but consumeStream replaces resp.Usage on each delta instead of accumulating it. In the default streaming path, the final Response therefore drops the earlier input token counts and retains only the last usage fragment. Anthropic's streaming code also never sets Total, so even if the fragments were merged the run-level total token count would remain zero. This leaves llm.response, session.end, and any cost/accounting derived from streaming Anthropic responses inconsistent with the non-streaming provider path.
Suggested fix: Accumulate streaming usage fragments in consumeStream instead of overwriting them, and have the Anthropic streaming path emit enough data to compute a correct final TokenUsage.Total, or emit the full merged usage on the terminal delta. Add a regression test that simulates Anthropic-style split usage deltas and asserts the final Response and emitted response event preserve input, output, cache, and total token counts.
title: correctness: anthropic streaming usage loses input tokens across deltas

acceptance: A session whose chat spans all have known cost preserves a root invoke_agent ddx.cost.amount equal to the sum of child costs even when provenance differs across turns; the root span only emits ddx.cost.source=unknown when at least one contributing chat span has unknown cost; automated tests cover mixed provider-reported/configured cost rollups.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: loop.go:289
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: The new root-span cost rollup logic marks the invoke_agent span as unknown whenever known child chat spans disagree on ddx.cost.source, currency, or pricing_ref. That drops a valid total for mixed but fully known sessions, which violates CONTRACT-001's rule that the root cost only becomes unknown when a contributing chat span has unknown cost. Reproduced against the current code by running a two-turn session with a provider-reported first turn and an exact configured-cost second turn: result.CostUSD=0.03 while the root span still emits ddx.cost.source=unknown.
Suggested fix: Roll up root ddx.cost.amount whenever every contributing chat span has known cost, and only force ddx.cost.source=unknown when a contributing span is actually unknown. If metadata such as pricing_ref or source cannot be represented cleanly at the root, omit or summarize those fields rather than discarding the known total. Add a regression test covering mixed known-cost provenance across turns.
title: correctness: preserve known root cost rollups across mixed known cost sources

acceptance: When a provider implements SessionStartMetadata but not ChatStartMetadata and Chat returns an error, the ended chat span records ddx.provider.system with that provider identity instead of unknown, while OpenAI-compatible providers still record resolved server.address and server.port.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: loop.go:182
Category: integration
Severity: medium
Description: The new chat-span bootstrap path always sources ddx.provider.system from chatStartIdentity. When a provider does not implement ChatStartMetadata, chatStartIdentity returns "unknown" instead of preserving the existing session-level provider identity. Before this commit, StartChat seeded ProviderSystem from sessionProvider, so failed calls from providers that only implement SessionStartMetadata still retained a meaningful identity. This change regresses those error spans to "unknown". The repo already has such a provider in provider/virtual/virtual.go, which exposes SessionStartMetadata but not ChatStartMetadata.
Suggested fix: Make chatStartIdentity fall back to sessionStartIdentity(provider) for providerSystem when ChatStartMetadata is unavailable, while still leaving server address and port unset unless the richer metadata hook is implemented.
title: integration: preserve provider identity fallback for providers without ChatStartMetadata

acceptance: A JSON payload using the DDx envelope field `title` but missing required `id` and `prompt` fails with an invalid prompt envelope error for -p, stdin, and @file input paths, while non-envelope JSON objects like `{"kind":"note","text":"hello"}` still pass through as raw prompt text; regression tests cover both behaviors.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: cmd/ddx-agent/main.go:381
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: `isPromptEnvelopeProbe` now treats `kind` plus `prompt`, `id`, `inputs`, `response_schema`, or `callback` as an envelope candidate, but it still ignores the optional `title` field even though `ddxPromptEnvelope` accepts and emits it. A malformed DDx payload like `{"kind":"prompt","title":"Inspect main"}` is therefore treated as raw prompt text instead of failing validation, so execution falls through to provider selection on all entry paths. Repro: `go run ./cmd/ddx-agent -p '{"kind":"prompt","title":"Inspect main"}' -provider nonexistent` returns `config: unknown provider "nonexistent"` instead of `invalid prompt envelope`; the same happens for stdin and `@file` because all three routes call `resolvePrompt` -> `parsePromptInput`.\nSuggested fix: include `title` in the probe heuristic for recognized envelope fields, and add regression coverage showing that `kind`+`title` without required `id`/`prompt` fails for inline, stdin, and `@file` inputs while non-envelope JSON such as `{"kind":"note","text":"hello"}` still passes through as raw prompt text.
title: correctness: reject malformed prompt envelopes with only title

acceptance: Network error during streaming causes consumeStream to return a non-nil error; partial content is not treated as a successful response; tests cover error propagation from both OpenAI and Anthropic providers
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: provider/openai/openai.go:213-244, provider/anthropic/anthropic.go:219-271
Category: bug
Severity: high
Description: Both ChatStream goroutines iterate stream.Next() and then unconditionally send Done:true. If stream.Next() returns false due to a network error, stream.Err() is never checked. The goroutine sends Done:true and the channel closes normally. consumeStream returns a partial Response with no error, and the agent loop treats it as a successful response. This silently corrupts tool call arguments or truncates content.
Suggested fix: After the stream loop exits, check stream.Err(). If non-nil, send a StreamDelta with an embedded error signal OR close the channel and handle via a separate error channel. Simplest approach: add an Error field to StreamDelta, check it in consumeStream, and return the error.
title: bug: stream errors swallowed in OpenAI and Anthropic ChatStream

acceptance: A JSON payload intended as a DDx prompt envelope but missing the required id field fails with an invalid prompt envelope error for -p, stdin, and @file input paths, while non-envelope JSON objects still pass through as raw prompt text; regression tests cover both behaviors.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: cmd/ddx-agent/main.go:331
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: The new probe heuristic only treats JSON as a DDx prompt envelope when `kind` is paired with `id`, `inputs`, `response_schema`, or `callback`. A malformed envelope like {"kind":"prompt","prompt":"Read main.go"} is therefore treated as raw prompt text instead of failing validation, so execution proceeds to provider selection. This violates the structured-I/O contract in the PRD and leaves invalid envelope input non-deterministic. Repro: `go run ./cmd/ddx-agent -p '{"kind":"prompt","prompt":"Read main.go"}' -provider nonexistent` returns `config: unknown provider "nonexistent"` instead of `invalid prompt envelope`.\nSuggested fix: Treat objects that contain `kind` plus `prompt` as envelope candidates too, so missing required `id` still reaches envelope validation; add regression coverage for missing-id envelopes across inline, stdin, and @file inputs.
title: correctness: reject malformed prompt envelopes missing id

acceptance: If attempt cost provenance is unknown, llm.response and session.end do not emit a positive cost_usd derived from DefaultPricing; known provider-reported or configured costs still propagate; regression tests cover known-cost and unknown-cost cases.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review of commit de8c772.
File: loop.go:219, loop.go:241, provider/openai/openai.go:83, provider/anthropic/anthropic.go:90, pricing.go:12
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: This change introduces structured cost provenance on resp.Attempt.Cost and explicitly marks both providers as source=unknown, but the runtime still unconditionally computes cost_usd from DefaultPricing and emits it on every successful llm.response. That produces internally contradictory telemetry: the same event can say the attempt cost source is unknown while also publishing a numeric USD value from a static fallback table. CONTRACT-001 explicitly forbids generic stale pricing tables as a fallback and requires unknown cost to remain unknown when no reported or configured cost exists.
Suggested fix: Stop deriving per-attempt and session cost from DefaultPricing once attempt-level cost provenance exists. Use provider-reported or configured runtime pricing only when available, otherwise omit numeric cost and keep the rollup unknown. Add tests that assert unknown-cost attempts do not emit positive cost_usd values.
title: correctness: llm.response cost_usd conflicts with unknown attempt cost provenance
